petitioner_background: The petitioner is a Canadian women's soccer coach (age 37 at time of filing) who sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in athletics under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act. At the time of filing on January 14, 2005, he was working as head coach of the University of Montana women's soccer team. His background includes prior work as a competitive soccer player in Canada until the mid-1990s, completion of Canada's National Coaching Institute Level 4 and Level 5 certification, an 'A License' from the Canadian Soccer Association, a U.S. Soccer Federation National 'A' Coaching License, and certification as a Strength and Conditioning Specialist. He previously coached at the University of North Dakota and served as an assistant coach with the Canadian Women's National Team program.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted evidence that as a soccer player he was named to the 'All Canadian Team' in Soccer in November 1992 and received provincial recognition from Soccer Nova Scotia when named Male Athlete of the Year in April 1992. He also submitted an undated letter from the President of NRT Sport Management Group (former coach of the Canadian Women's National Team 1986-1991 and 1995-1999) stating that the petitioner received a 'Petro Canada Olympic Torch Scholarship Award' in 1995 and 1996 for coaching.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the player awards insufficient because the petitioner stopped competing in the mid-1990s and the field in which he seeks to continue work is coaching, not competitive athletics. Citing Lee v. I.N.S., 237 F. Supp. 2d 914, 918 (N.D. Ill. 2002), the AAO held that competitive athletics and coaching are not the same area of expertise and that the petitioner must demonstrate sustained acclaim based on his achievements as a coach. No evidence showed that teams coached by the petitioner won nationally or internationally recognized prizes or awards. Regarding the Petro Canada Olympic Torch Scholarship, the AAO found no primary or secondary evidence corroborating receipt (citing 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1) and (2)), noted that the letter claiming receipt was issued years later, and further found that a scholarship represents funding to pursue further education and training rather than a nationally or internationally recognized award for excellence in the field.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted evidence that as a player he represented Canada at the 1993 World University Games. He also submitted an April 1997 certificate from the National Coaching Institute (NCI), Victoria, Canada showing he completed a diploma program in high performance coaching; two letters from the NCI Director (June 24, 2003 and April 23, 2004) explaining that he was enrolled from September 1995 - April 1996, earned Honours in 8 of 12 sports science modules, received Level 4 certification, subsequently completed Level 5 (held by fewer than 8 coaches in all of Canada), and was one of only 22 soccer coaches in Canada to complete the NCCP Level IV Diploma Program. Also submitted: a March 30, 1999 letter from the CSA Director of Coaching and Player Development reflecting receipt of an 'A License'; a 2002 National 'A' Coaching License from the U.S. Soccer Federation; a September 23, 2003 Certified Strength and Conditioning Specialist certificate; a letter from the Head Soccer Coach at University of Nebraska stating petitioner is one of only 2 coaches in North American soccer holding Canadian Level 5, Canadian 'A' License, and American 'A' License. Petitioner also submitted evidence of his role as an assistant coach with the Canadian Women's National Team (per letters, from 1996 through the Algarve Cup 2000 in Portugal), including an April 9, 1999 Daily News article noting he accompanied the Canadian team to Australia for the three-team Australian Cup. Counsel on appeal referred to an unpublished AAO decision involving an ice skating coach for the Russian Olympic Team.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that his participation as a player at the 1993 World University Games could not serve because the field sought is coaching, not playing. The AAO held that earning a coaching diploma, completing Level 4 and Level 5 certification, obtaining 'A' coaching licenses in Canada and the U.S., and CSCS certification do not constitute 'membership in associations in the field' requiring 'outstanding achievements of their members.' The AAO emphasized that these credentials reflect completion of educational programs and that the NCI selected petitioner based on leadership, intelligence, and willingness to work hard — not based on past outstanding achievements as an elite coach. Regarding the Canadian National Team work, the AAO found inconsistencies: petitioner was employed at University of North Dakota from April 1999 to December 2000, undermining claims he worked with the national team 'through to the Algarve Cup 2000.' Citing Matter of Caron International, 19 I&N Dec. 791, 795 (Commr. 1988), the AAO gave less weight to questionable expert statements. The AAO distinguished the unpublished ice skating decision, noting no evidence of petitioner's membership on an Olympic team and that assistant coaching at the three-team Australian Cup in 1999 is not consistent with sustained acclaim at the top of the field. Per 8 C.F.R. § 103.3(c), unpublished decisions are not binding. The AAO also noted that the criterion requires 'membership in associations' (plural), and a single membership that terminated years before filing does not meet the plain language.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted several articles, including two articles printed in Daily News that were primarily about him and his work as a coach. One of the two articles did not include a date of publication.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that only two articles were primarily about the petitioner, one of which lacked a date of publication as required by the regulatory criterion. Further, no evidence such as circulation statistics was submitted to show that Daily News, a local newspaper serving Nanaimo, British Columbia, qualifies as a professional or major trade publication or other major media.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel argued on appeal that articles authored by the petitioner and his ball striking video 'Breaking the Rules' meet this criterion. Petitioner submitted a letter from an NCI official stating petitioner's training methods are 'integral contributions to the fitness component of the sport of soccer' and that a Dr. [unnamed] adopted petitioner's strength and conditioning materials for use with his clients and taught them at NCI. Also submitted: a letter from a CSA official stating 'We have utilised [petitioner's] programs and videos in our development of Canada's best youth players' and referencing the ball striking video 'Breaking the Rules'; a letter from the Vancouver Whitecaps Women's Assistant Coach/Head Coach of Girls Development stating he uses petitioner's information daily and that the Ball Striking and Technical Videos provide a visual development tool; a letter from the Program Coordinator/Head Coach of Pacific Soccer Academy stating petitioner developed the curriculum and acts as Chief Consultant, provided a copy of his Ball Striking Video used to instruct student-athletes and new coaches. Petitioner also submitted evidence of being 1 of 10 coaches invited to the February 24-26, 2006 Elite College Soccer Coaches Summit in Lincoln, Nebraska, with a letter from the Head Women's Soccer Coach at University of North Carolina (who led the Tar Heels to 18 Collegiate National Championships) stating each coach returned with one of petitioner's ball striking videos.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held that articles authored by petitioner belong under the separate 'scholarly articles' criterion at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(vi), and that the regulatory criteria are separate and distinct — treating them as interchangeable would render the requirement of meeting at least three criteria meaningless. The NCI official's letter failed to identify the specific 'strength and conditioning materials' at issue, did not name the 'top athletes' using the regimes or the extent of use, and lacked a confirming letter from the Dr. himself; moreover, since petitioner attended NCI, its use of his material does not show acclaim beyond his alma mater. The AAO accepted that contributions must be not only original but also of 'major significance,' a phrase it presumed was not superfluous. While the CSA and Whitecaps/Academy letters indicated use of petitioner's videos and programs, there was no evidence these developmental materials were of major significance in the field — for example, no evidence that a large number of copies of the videos had been sold nationwide, or that the material had significant impact beyond his affiliated organizations. The Coaches Summit evidence and UNC coach letter were dismissed because these events occurred after the January 14, 2005 filing date, citing 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak, 14 I&N Dec. 45, 49 (Commr. 1971); further, nothing in those letters indicated the coaches were aware of the video before the conference or considered it a contribution of major significance.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted articles he authored appearing in SSR, Performance Conditioning for Soccer newsletter, The Complete Guide to Soccer Conditioning, and How to Improve the 7 Speeds of Soccer - Beginner and Intermediate Players.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that aside from the article in SSR, there was no indication that the remaining articles were 'scholarly' in nature. Further, no evidence (such as circulation statistics) was submitted to show that any of these publications qualified as professional or major trade publications or other major media.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted letters asserting his involvement as 'Coach of Canada's National Women's Team,' including a letter stating that 'as assistant national team coach [the petitioner] worked with the national team athletes at all age levels from 1996 through to the Algarve Cup 2000 in Portugal.' He submitted a May 9, 2006 internet printout from canadasoccer.com identifying another individual as Head Coach of the 'Women's U-20 Team' and noting that since 2000 that individual served as an assistant coach to the Canadian Women's National Team Head Coach, and identifying U-18, U-17, U-16, and U-15 'Development Teams.' Petitioner also submitted evidence of serving as head coach at University of Montana and University of North Dakota and assistant coach at University of Southern California. He submitted a February 8, 2006 article entitled 'Griz soccer signs six to National Letters of Intent' showing his 2005 Montana squad finished 3-13-1 overall and 1-5 in Big Sky Conference play.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO accepted that the Canadian National Women's Team has a distinguished reputation but found the letters offered insufficient information about the nature and importance of petitioner's role compared to other assistant coaches — noting, for example, that another assistant coach had been with the team since 1997 and 'helped guide the team to the 1999 Women's World Cup.' Aside from the three-team Australian Cup, the record lacked supporting evidence of specific competitions petitioner attended. The AAO found that involvement with U-18, U-17, U-16, and U-15 'Development Teams' is not tantamount to a leading or critical role for the 'national' team. Regarding the universities, the AAO found no competitive statistics or evidence establishing that Montana, North Dakota, or USC women's soccer teams had distinguished reputations during his tenure — no NCAA Division I rankings showing his teams finished among top teams nationally (such as NCAA Sweet 16 appearances) or evidence of regional conference tournament wins. The AAO contrasted this with the University of Nebraska (eight Sweet 16 appearances in nine years, 162-40-8 record, three Big 12 regular season crowns, five Big 12 Tournament titles) and University of North Carolina (18 of 24 national championships in women's collegiate soccer history). Petitioner's 2005 Montana squad's 3-13-1 overall record further undermined the claim.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner failed to demonstrate receipt of a major internationally recognized award or to meet at least three of the regulatory criteria at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3). Review of the record did not establish that the petitioner had distinguished himself to such an extent that he had achieved sustained national or international acclaim or was within the small percentage at the very top of his field. The evidence was not persuasive that the petitioner's achievements set him significantly above almost all others in his field at the national or international level. Therefore, the petitioner did not establish eligibility pursuant to section 203(b)(1)(A)(i) of the Act, and per section 291 of the Act, the burden of proof remains entirely with the petitioner, which he failed to sustain.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Lee v. I.N.S., 237 F. Supp. 2d 914, 918 (N.D. Ill. 2002)
Matter of Caron International, 19 I&N Dec. 791, 795 (Commr. 1988)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO noted a credibility/evidentiary inconsistency: a supporting letter claimed petitioner worked with the Canadian National Team 'through to the Algarve Cup 2000 in Portugal,' but petitioner's own Form G-325A showed he was employed at the University of North Dakota from April 1999 to December 2000, so the AAO gave that expert statement less weight under Matter of Caron International. The AAO also noted the absence of primary and secondary evidence corroborating receipt of the Petro Canada Olympic Torch Scholarship Award, creating a presumption of ineligibility under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(2). Additionally, evidence of the 2006 Elite College Coaches Summit and related video distribution was excluded as post-filing evidence under Matter of Katigbak, since a petitioner must establish eligibility at the time of filing.
